Suppose all five premises are true at once.

Then there exists a setting where people are trying to distinguish weak scientific explanations from strong ones. In that setting, grammar and coherence filters are available, but they do not help much, because most weak explanations are already grammatical and coherent. So grammaticality and local coherence are poor separators.

At the same time, the system used to evaluate or generate explanations may be optimized aggressively for current human judgments. If so, it tends to remove candidates that look strange now but would later count as breakthroughs. Therefore the search procedure has a bias: it preserves what present evaluators endorse and suppresses some of what future science would have needed.

Now add the claim about relativity’s 289 unique words. If those words may not matter at all, then lexical novelty is also a poor separator. A breakthrough can be expressed largely through familiar vocabulary if the novelty lies in relations among concepts. So a candidate need not look unusual at the word level in order to be revolutionary.

That leaves an awkward picture. Grammar does not distinguish much. Coherence does not distinguish much. Lexical variety may not distinguish much. Yet aggressive optimization for present judgment still removes some of the very things that matter. So the dangerous signal is not mere bad writing, and the valuable signal is not mere unusual wording. The decisive factor must be some deeper pattern of conceptual relation that current judges may misread, especially when it appears in forms that seem odd.

The last two premises guarantee that at least some strings in the environment are:

“Gathered undershirts pilch foraminifer comminuting.”

and

“Slubbering lethargically sheared distributed unduke.”

Assuming they are present as candidates, examples, or noise, they show one trivial case where grammar and coherence likely fail in the opposite direction: some outputs are plainly bizarre. But this does not rescue the filters, because the earlier premise already states that most weak scientific explanations are grammatical and coherent. So eliminating obvious gibberish still leaves the main problem untouched. These strings are easy rejects; the hard cases are the fluent but empty explanations and the weird-but-potentially-profound candidates.

Because lexical novelty may be irrelevant, the oddity of words like “pilch,” “foraminifer,” “slubbering,” and “unduke” also tells us little by itself. A strange vocabulary can mark nonsense, but novelty in science need not. Conversely, a real breakthrough can arrive in ordinary words. So any procedure that rewards familiar wording and penalizes strangeness, while also relying on grammar and coherence, will be well tuned to accept polished mediocrity, reject obvious gibberish, and risk discarding some future breakthroughs.

The world implied by these premises is one where the easy screens mostly remove only the easy failures, while the central difficulty remains: the difference between a weak explanation and a transformative one often lives neither in syntax nor in surface fluency nor in unusual word counts, but in relations current human judgment may not yet know how to value.
